concepts.title: Applications
concepts.conceptBody:
Dipole Moments

A dipole consists of a positive and an equal negative charge separated by a distance within a molecule. The degree of polarity of a bond is given by the dipole moment $(\mu )$, which is the product of either charge (e) and the distance (d) between them. $\mu =$ de. 'e' is of the order of magnitude of the electronic charge, i.e. about ${{10}^{-10}}\text{esu}$ and $\text{d}$ is the distance between the atomic centres, i.e., about ${{10}^{-8}}~\text{cm}$. Hence dipole moment may be expected to have values around ${{10}^{-10}}{{10}^{-8}}$ $={{10}^{-18}}$ esu-cm. It is however, general practice to express dipole moments in Debye units (D), $1\text{D}={{10}^{-18}}$ esu-cm.

If the charge is in SI units (Coulombs) and $d$ in metre $\mu $ will coulomb-metre (C.m) units.

$1\text{D}=3.336\quad {{10}^{-30}}\text{C}.\text{m}\text{. }$

Dipole moment is a vector quantity , i.e., it has both magnitude as well as direction. Thus the overall value of the dipole moment of a polar molecule depends on its geometry and shape i.e., vectorial addition of dipole moment of constituent bonds.

Any covalent bond which has a certain degree of polarity will have a corresponding dipole moment, though it does not follow that compounds containing such bonds will have dipole moments, for the polarity of the molecule as a whole is the vector sum of the individual bond moments. For example, $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ has zero dipole moment, although the $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond is a polar bond. This shows that $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is a linear molecule, $\text{O}=\text{C}=\text{O}$, so that the dipole moments of the two $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bonds cancel out. The $\text{C}\to \text{Cl}$ bond has a definite polarity and a definite dipole moment but carbon tetrachloride has zero dipole moment because it is a tetrahedral molecule, and the resultant of the $4\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond moments is zero. On the contrary $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{Cl},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ and $\text{CHC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ have definite dipole moments.

Applications of Dipole Moment

(i) To decide polarity of the molecule: Molecules having zero dipole moment are said to be non-polar molecules and those having ${{\mu }_{R}}\ne 0$ are polar in nature.

(ii) To determine geometry of molecules: The values of dipole moments provide valuable information about the structure of molecules.

(a) $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}},\text{C}{{\text{S}}_{2}}$ molecules are linear as values of their dipole moments are zero.

(b) ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is not a linear molecule as it has dipole moment. Actually, it has V-shaped structure and the bond angle is 105 . Similarly, $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ has a bent structure.

(c) In ammonia, three hydrogen atoms do not lie symmetrically with respect to nitrogen as it has dipole moment. It has pyramidal structure.

(iii) To distinguish cis and trans forms of geometrical isomers: Experimental values of dipole moment of the isomers (cis and trans) are determined. The trans isomer usually possesses either zero dipole moment or very low value in comparison to cis form.

It should be noted that if two groups have opposite inductive character then trans isomer will have greater dipole moment of,

${{\mu }_{\text{Trans }}}>{{\mu }_{\text{cis }}}$

(iv) To determine orientation in benzene ring: Dipole moment is useful to ascertain the orientation of substituents. The greater the dipole moment, the greater is the asymmetry. In general, dipole moment follows the order:

Ortho > meta > para

Example 4

Both $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}$ are linear but dipole moment of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ in zero but for ${{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}$ it is non-zero, why?

Solution. 

The answer lies in the structure of these molecules. $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is a symmetrical molecule while ${{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is unsymmetrical. Thus for ${{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}$, dipoles do not cancel each other, leaving the molecule with a resultant dipole moment, while the bond moment of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ cancel each other, so $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ has no net dipole moment.
